1: A shadow: 'O LORD my God, in you do I take refuge; save me from all my pursuers and deliver me.' The fugitive's cry for divine protection. The superscription connects to Cush the Benjaminite — a false accusation episode in David's life. As a shadow: Jesus alone was pursued by accusers whose charges were entirely false (Matthew 26:59-60: 'the chief priests and the whole council were seeking false testimony against Jesus'). The innocent fugitive who takes refuge in YHWH is the template Christ inhabits at his trial.
2: A shadow: 'lest like a lion they tear my soul apart, rending it in pieces, with none to deliver.' The lion tearing as the image of enemy destruction. Peter uses the same image for the devil: 'your adversary the devil prowls around like a roaring lion, seeking someone to devour' (1 Peter 5:8). At the cross, the lion was unleashed — and the one torn was Christ, so that those who take refuge in him are not devoured.
3: A shadow: 'O LORD my God, if I have done this, if there is wrong in my hands' — the protestation of innocence. Jesus alone could make this claim before YHWH with absolute truthfulness. Pilate's declaration 'I find no guilt in him' (John 18:38) is the judicial echo of the psalmist's claim. The one who bore the world's guilt was the one who had committed none of his own — the substitution requires this precise innocence.
4: A shadow: 'if I have repaid my friend with evil or plundered my enemy without cause...' — the negative covenant oath. Christ's covenant faithfulness was total: he never repaid evil with evil (1 Peter 2:23: 'when he was reviled, he did not revile in return') and never plundered unjustly. The perfection of his human covenant-keeping is what makes him the acceptable substitute for those who have broken every covenant term.
5: A shadow: 'let the enemy pursue my soul and overtake it, and let him trample my life to the ground and lay my glory in the dust.' The ultimate curse-formula — what the psalmist says should happen if he is guilty. Christ voluntarily entered this curse for those who were guilty: 'he made him to be sin who knew no sin' (2 Corinthians 5:21). The glory laid in the dust is the incarnation and crucifixion; the resurrection is the divine vindication that proves the guilt was transferred, not inherent.
6: A revelation of God: 'arise, O LORD, in your anger; lift yourself up against the fury of my enemies; awake for me; you have appointed a judgment.' The petition for divine arising to judge — the 'arise, O LORD' cry echoes the Sinai prayer (Numbers 10:35). The divine judgment that Ps 7 calls for is what Christ's return inaugurates: the appointed judge (Acts 17:31) rises to render the verdict that history has been waiting for.
7: A revelation of God: 'let the assembly of the peoples be gathered about you; over it return on high.' The universal assembly gathered around YHWH — the eschatological vision: all peoples assembled before the divine throne. Revelation 20:11-12 depicts this: 'a great white throne and him who was seated on it... and I saw the dead, great and small, standing before the throne.' The Judge of Ps 7 is the Christ of Rev 20.
8: A theme: 'the LORD judges the peoples; judge me, O LORD, according to my righteousness and according to the integrity that is in me.' The appeal to divine justice — which is also the appeal to divine mercy for those who have no righteousness of their own. Paul's argument in Romans is precisely that no one can say 'judge me according to my righteousness' — except the one who has Christ's righteousness imputed to them. The prayer of Psalms 7:8 becomes safe only for those in whom Christ's integrity stands.
9: A revelation of God: 'oh, let the evil of the wicked come to an end, and may you establish the righteous — you who test minds and hearts, O righteous God!' The searching of minds and hearts by the righteous God — the divine omniscience applied to moral judgment. 'The heart is deceitful above all things... who can understand it? I the LORD search the heart and test the mind' (Jeremiah 17:9-10). Jesus, who 'knew what was in man' (John 2:25), exercises this divine prerogative as the Son who knows the Father.
10: A theme: 'my shield is with God, who saves the upright in heart.' The divine shield again (cf. Genesis 15:1; Psalms 3:3) — YHWH as protector of the upright. Christ is both the shield and the one who makes the upright: 'the upright in heart' are those whose hearts have been transformed by the Spirit (Ezekiel 36:26; Psalms 51:10) so that they may stand in the judgment Ps 7 describes.
11: A revelation of God: 'God is a righteous judge, and a God who feels indignation every day.' The perpetual divine indignation at injustice — YHWH does not grow accustomed to sin. This perpetual moral attentiveness is what makes the atonement necessary: every sin is registered by the one who 'feels indignation every day.' The cross is where this daily indignation is absorbed in full, so that those in Christ stand before the righteous judge without condemnation (Romans 8:1).
12: A revelation of God: 'if a man does not repent, God will whet his sword; he has bent and readied his bow.' The divine warning: the sword and bow of divine judgment are prepared for the unrepentant. The urgency of repentance — what the NT calls 'today' (Hebrews 3:7,13,15: 'today, if you hear his voice, do not harden your hearts') is the urgency of Psalms 7:12. The prepared sword is not yet swung; the repentance that turns it is available now through Christ.
13: A revelation of God: 'he has prepared for him his deadly weapons, making his arrows fiery shafts.' The divine warrior's arsenal — the weapons of the holy war are prepared specifically for the unrepentant. Yet Christ takes the fire: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us' (Galatians 3:13) — the fiery shaft of divine judgment struck the Son so that it need not strike those who take refuge in him.
14: A theme: 'behold, the wicked man conceives evil and is pregnant with mischief and gives birth to lies.' The wicked person's internal trajectory — evil conceived, born as mischief, delivered as lies. James 1:15 describes sin's similar trajectory: 'desire when it has conceived gives birth to sin, and sin when it is fully grown brings forth death.' Christ enters the world precisely where this trajectory has led: a world full of the death that sin-conceived-in-mischief produces.
15: A shadow: 'he makes a pit, digging it out, and falls into the hole he has made.' The self-trapping principle of evil — the pit-digger falls into his own pit (Proverbs 26:27; Ecclesiastes 10:8). Applied to the crucifixion: those who plotted to destroy Jesus through death were unwittingly using the instrument of their own defeat. The resurrection is the moment when the enemy falls into the pit: death is defeated by death (Hebrews 2:14: 'through death he might destroy the one who has the power of death, that is, the devil').
16: A shadow: 'his mischief returns upon his own head, and on his own skull his violence descends.' The law of return: violence against the innocent returns on the violent. The cross applies this: YHWH allowed the violence of crucifixion to fall on the innocent Son — and it returned on all who hold the power of sin and death through the resurrection. Satan's greatest weapon (death) became his own defeat.
17: A revelation of God: 'I will give to the LORD the thanks due to his righteousness, and I will sing praise to the name of the LORD, the Most High.' The psalm ends in doxology — as most penitential-vindication psalms do. The pattern (crisis → lament → confidence → praise) is the structure of the gospel itself: the crisis of human sin → the lament of the cross → the confidence of the resurrection → the eternal praise of those who are vindicated in Christ. The 'name of the LORD, the Most High' is the name now borne by Jesus: 'the name that is above every name' (Philippians 2:9).
